enrichment.short_description: GlobaLeaks is a free, open-source platform that lets NGOs, newsrooms, companies and public bodies run secure, anonymous whistleblowing and reporting channels over Tor and HTTPS.
enrichment.seo_title: GlobaLeaks Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GlobaLeaks review 2026: how this open-source Tor-based whistleblowing platform works, its security model, pricing (its free), key features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GlobaLeaks is a free and open-source software platform purpose-built to let any organization, from small NGOs to national media outlets and public administrations, stand up a secure, anonymous whistleblowing and reporting channel without writing code. Rather than being a hosted SaaS product, GlobaLeaks is software you deploy yourself: it runs as a web application, typically exposed both over standard HTTPS and as a Tor Onion Service, so that a source can submit a tip, documents, or a message while remaining as anonymous as the underlying network allows. The project positions itself as infrastructure for accountability journalism, corporate compliance with anti-corruption and whistleblower-protection laws, and civil-society human-rights reporting, and it has been formally recognized as a Digital Public Good by the Digital Public Goods Alliance because of its transparent development model and privacy-first design. The idea for GlobaLeaks was conceived on 15 December 2010 by Fabio Pietrosanti and shared within the hacktivist and transparency-advocacy community; the first working prototype followed on 6 September 2011. Early core contributors included Arturo Filasto, Claudio Agosti, Giovanni Pellerano and Michele Orru, several of whom were already active in the Tor and digital-rights ecosystem. The project has been stewarded ever since by the Hermes Center for Transparency and Digital Human Rights, an Italian nonprofit organization dedicated to freedom of expression and digital civil liberties online, which continues to coordinate GlobaLeaks development, security audits and documentation as a community and grant-funded effort rather than a venture-backed company. Because GlobaLeaks has never taken equity investment, its funding history is a matter of public grant disclosure rather than Crunchbase-style financing rounds. In 2012 the GlobaLeaks 2.0 and companion Tor2web 3.0 releases were funded with roughly 108,400 US dollars under a Freedom2Connect-linked program. In 2014 the Hermes Center was awarded 200,000 euro from a foundation supporting the deployment of whistleblowing initiatives in the Global South, and later that year the Open Technology Fund committed approximately 234,000 US dollars to fund a GlobaLeaks development roadmap running from Q3 2014 through Q1 2016. The project publishes its funders and progress reports openly on its website as part of its transparency commitment, which mirrors the transparency it asks of the organizations that deploy it. Architecturally, GlobaLeaks is a Python-based backend paired with a browser-based frontend that an administrator configures through a setup wizard, defining reporting channels, recipient roles, and intake questionnaires without needing development skills. Security is central to the design: submissions and attachments are encrypted using the GlobaLeaks encryption protocol, communications between whistleblowers and recipients are end-to-end protected, and the platform enforces TLS 1.3 with an SSL Labs A+ configuration, including automatic certificate issuance and renewal through Let's Encrypt. Deployments are strongly encouraged to run as a Tor Onion Service (v3), since Tor's design resists selective interception and censorship far better than plain HTTPS, making it much harder for a network operator or state actor to identify or block a specific whistleblower. On the data-handling side, GlobaLeaks follows a strict minimization policy: it does not log source IP addresses, browser fingerprints, or device information, does not embed third-party trackers or persistent cookies, and is built to delete submission data as soon as retention policy allows rather than storing it indefinitely. Core features include configurable multi-step intake questionnaires, role-based recipient and reviewer workflows, secure two-way messaging between source and recipient without ever revealing the source's identity, file upload support for documents and evidence, multi-language interfaces spanning dozens of locales, granular administrative controls for defining multiple independent reporting channels within one instance, and audit logging for administrators that stops short of compromising source anonymity. Because it is open source software released without a commercial license, there is no pricing tier structure in the traditional SaaS sense: GlobaLeaks itself is free to download, self-host and modify under its open-source license, and the real cost to an adopting organization is server hosting, systems-administration time, and optionally paid implementation or hardening support from third-party consultancies or the Hermes Center's partner network, some of whom offer managed hosting for GlobaLeaks instances. The target audience spans investigative journalism outlets and media organizations that need SecureDrop-style leak channels, NGOs and human-rights groups operating in high-risk or authoritarian environments, and increasingly corporate compliance and ethics teams and public-sector bodies that must comply with whistleblower-protection regulation such as the EU Whistleblowing Directive, which requires many employers to provide a confidential reporting channel. Competitively, GlobaLeaks sits alongside SecureDrop as one of the two best-known open-source, security-audited whistleblowing platforms, but it differentiates itself with an admin-configurable multi-tenant model that lets one deployment serve multiple departments or reporting channels, a strong focus on ease of self-hosted setup via a guided wizard, and an explicit compliance angle aimed at organizations that need an auditable, GDPR-aligned reporting tool rather than only investigative-journalism leak drops. Its main trade-off versus commercial whistleblowing SaaS vendors such as EQS Integrity Line, Whistle, or NAVEX is that GlobaLeaks requires the adopting organization to own hosting, security patching and uptime themselves, in exchange for full data sovereignty, no vendor lock-in, and zero licensing cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GlobaLeaks is free, open-source software for building secure and anonymous whistleblowing or reporting channels. Instead of signing up for a hosted service, an organization downloads and deploys the GlobaLeaks application on its own server or a partner's managed infrastructure, then configures reporting workflows through an administrative setup wizard.

The project was started in December 2010 by Fabio Pietrosanti and shared with the transparency and hacktivist community, with a first working prototype released in September 2011. It has since been developed and maintained by the Hermes Center for Transparency and Digital Human Rights, an Italian nonprofit, and is recognized by the Digital Public Goods Alliance as a Digital Public Good.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GlobaLeaks runs as a Tor Onion Service (v3) alongside standard HTTPS access, encrypts submissions and attachments end to end using its own encryption protocol, and enforces TLS 1.3 with automatic Let's Encrypt certificates. It deliberately avoids logging IP addresses, device fingerprints, or browser metadata, and does not embed third-party trackers or persistent cookies.

Administrators can define multiple independent reporting channels with custom intake questionnaires, assign recipients and reviewers by role, and communicate two-way with a source through secure messaging without ever learning that source's identity. The interface supports dozens of languages, making it usable for multinational NGOs, newsrooms and enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GlobaLeaks itself carries no license fee: it is free, open-source software that any organization can self-host, audit and modify. There is no SaaS subscription tier structure because GlobaLeaks is not sold as a hosted product by its maintainers.

The real cost of adoption is infrastructure and operations: server hosting, systems-administration time, and optionally paid implementation, hardening or managed-hosting support from third-party consultancies or partners in the Hermes Center's network.

faqs.question: Is GlobaLeaks free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. GlobaLeaks is free, open-source software. There is no license fee; the only costs are the hosting infrastructure and administration time an organization spends running its own instance.

faqs.question: Who maintains GlobaLeaks?
faqs.answer: GlobaLeaks is developed and maintained by the Hermes Center for Transparency and Digital Human Rights, an Italian nonprofit, with funding from grants such as the Open Technology Fund rather than commercial investors.

faqs.question: Does GlobaLeaks require Tor to work?
faqs.answer: No, but it supports and recommends running as a Tor Onion Service in addition to standard HTTPS access, since Tor offers stronger resistance to network-level identification and censorship for sensitive sources.

faqs.question: Is GlobaLeaks suitable for corporate whistleblower compliance, not just journalism?
faqs.answer: Yes. Beyond investigative journalism, organizations use GlobaLeaks to meet whistleblower-protection regulations, such as the EU Whistleblowing Directive, by offering an internal, confidential reporting channel.

faqs.question: What data does GlobaLeaks store about a whistleblower?
faqs.answer: The platform is designed to avoid logging IP addresses, browser or device fingerprints, and persistent cookies, and it follows a strict data-retention policy that deletes submission data as soon as policy allows.

faqs.question: How is GlobaLeaks different from SecureDrop?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, security-focused whistleblowing platforms, but GlobaLeaks emphasizes an admin-configurable, multi-channel model with a guided setup wizard, making it approachable for compliance and NGO deployments as well as journalism leak drops.

enrichment.short_description: GLPI is an open-source IT asset management, helpdesk and service-desk platform used to track equipment, tickets, contracts and inventory across an organization.
enrichment.seo_title: GLPI Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GLPI review 2026: how this open-source IT asset management and helpdesk platform works, its pricing tiers from Teclib, key features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GLPI, short for Gestionnaire Libre de Parc Informatique or Free IT Equipment Manager, is an open-source IT service management platform that combines IT asset management, a help desk and ticketing system, and inventory tracking in a single PHP-based application. Organizations use it to keep a live inventory of computers, servers, network devices, software licenses and other assets, to run an ITIL-aligned service desk for incidents and requests, and to manage contracts, warranties and knowledge-base articles tied to that inventory. The GLPI community project began in 2003 under the direction of the INDEPNET association, led by Jean-Mathieu Doleans, Julien Dombre and Bazile Lebeau, making it one of the longer-running open-source IT management tools still in active development more than two decades later. Teclib', a French company founded in 2011, began as an integrator building on top of GLPI starting in 2009, and in the summer of 2015 the GLPI community's leadership transferred roadmap management and development leadership to Teclib', which has served as the project's commercial editor and primary maintainer ever since. Teclib' is headquartered in France, with offices including one in the Paris region, and continues to fund core GLPI engineering through its commercial GLPI Network cloud and support offerings while keeping the underlying software open source under the GNU General Public License. Because GLPI has never taken outside venture funding, its business model is closer to a traditional open-core software publisher than a startup: revenue comes from subscriptions, hosting, and professional services rather than equity financing rounds. Technically, GLPI is a PHP web application backed by a relational database, and it is commonly paired with the separate GLPI Agent, a lightweight inventory agent that can be deployed across a fleet of endpoints to automatically discover hardware, software and network information and feed it back into the central GLPI instance. The platform exposes both a browser-based administrative console for IT teams and a self-service portal where end users can submit tickets, browse the knowledge base, and track request status. Its architecture is extensible through a large plugin ecosystem covering areas like advanced reporting, mobile device management, data centre and rack management, and integrations with monitoring and directory services such as LDAP and Active Directory. Core features include asset and configuration management (CMDB-style tracking of hardware, software, licenses and consumables), an ITIL-based service desk supporting incidents, problems, changes and service requests, contract and financial tracking tied to assets, a searchable knowledge base, project and task management modules, customizable dashboards and reporting, and role-based access control for multi-department or multi-site organizations. Recent commercial tiers have also begun layering in AI-assisted features and advanced dashboards at higher subscription levels. GLPI's pricing follows a freemium open-core structure. The core GLPI Community edition remains free and open source for organizations willing to self-host and self-support it. For organizations that want managed infrastructure or professional support, Teclib' sells GLPI Network cloud subscriptions priced per IT agent per month: a Public Cloud tier starts around 19 euro per agent per month with a minimum of one agent, unlimited assets and end users, 5 gigabytes of shared disk space, daily backups and automatic updates, while a Private Cloud tier runs around 21 euro per agent per month with a minimum of 25 agents, 50 gigabytes of dedicated disk space, hourly backups, a VPN tunnel and IP filtering. For teams that prefer to self-host but still want vendor support, Teclib' also sells self-hosted subscription tiers: a Basic plan around 100 euro per month for up to 10 IT agents and 500 assets, a Standard plan around 300 euro per month for up to 50 agents and 5,000 assets that adds standard plugins, AI features and an advanced dashboard, and an Advanced plan around 1,000 euro per month for 50-plus agents that unlocks the full plugin catalogue and mail-based approval workflows. GLPI's target audience spans small IT teams that just need free, self-hosted asset tracking, mid-size organizations that want managed cloud hosting with support, and larger enterprises or managed service providers that need multi-site, multi-tenant deployments with extensive plugin customization. Competitively, GLPI is most often compared with other open-source ITSM tools such as Snipe-IT for pure asset tracking or osTicket for pure help desk, as well as commercial ITSM suites like Freshservice, Jira Service Management and ServiceNow. Its differentiation is combining asset management and service desk in one open, self-hostable codebase with two decades of community maturity, a large plugin marketplace, and a French vendor offering both managed cloud and self-hosted commercial support paths, giving adopters a lower-cost, less vendor-locked alternative to the large proprietary ITSM platforms while still offering a paid support path for organizations that need it.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GLPI is an open-source IT asset management and service desk platform, combining hardware and software inventory tracking with an ITIL-based ticketing system. The project began in 2003 under the INDEPNET association and has been developed and commercially published by the French company Teclib' since 2015.

Teclib' keeps the core software open source under the GPL while monetizing through GLPI Network, a set of managed-cloud and self-hosted subscription tiers priced per IT agent, plus professional services and a plugin ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GLPI tracks hardware, software licenses, network devices and consumables in a CMDB-style inventory, and pairs with the separate GLPI Agent to automatically discover assets across a fleet of endpoints. Its service desk covers incidents, requests, problems and changes in an ITIL-aligned workflow with a self-service portal for end users.

Additional modules cover contract and warranty tracking, a searchable knowledge base, project and task management, customizable dashboards, and role-based access for multi-site or multi-department organizations. A large plugin marketplace extends the platform into areas like data-centre and rack management, mobile device management, and advanced reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The GLPI Community edition is free and open source for self-hosted, self-supported use. Teclib' also sells GLPI Network managed-cloud tiers priced per IT agent per month, roughly 19 euro for Public Cloud (minimum one agent) and 21 euro for Private Cloud (minimum 25 agents), each with different backup, storage and networking allowances.

For teams that prefer to self-host with vendor support, self-hosted subscriptions start around 100 euro per month for a Basic tier (up to 10 agents, 500 assets), rising to roughly 300 euro per month for Standard (up to 50 agents, adds AI features and advanced dashboards) and around 1,000 euro per month for Advanced (50-plus agents, full plugin catalogue).

faqs.question: Is GLPI free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core GLPI Community edition is free and open source under the GPL for organizations that self-host and self-support it. Paid tiers add managed cloud hosting, support and extra features.

faqs.question: Who develops GLPI?
faqs.answer: GLPI began in 2003 under the INDEPNET association and has been developed and commercially published by the French company Teclib' since it took over project leadership in 2015.

faqs.question: What is the GLPI Agent?
faqs.answer: GLPI Agent is a separate, lightweight companion tool that automatically inventories hardware, software and network information on endpoints and reports it back to the central GLPI instance.

faqs.question: Can GLPI replace a dedicated help desk tool?
faqs.answer: Yes, GLPI includes a full ITIL-aligned service desk module covering incidents, requests, problems and changes, so many organizations use it as both their asset system and their help desk.

faqs.question: Does GLPI support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, GLPI ships with translations for numerous languages including English, French, Spanish, German, Italian and Portuguese.

faqs.question: What is the difference between GLPI Network Cloud and self-hosted paid plans?
faqs.answer: GLPI Network Cloud is a fully managed hosting subscription priced per IT agent, while the self-hosted paid tiers let you run GLPI on your own infrastructure while still getting Teclib' support and access to premium plugins.

enrichment.short_description: GlusterFS is an open-source, scale-out distributed file system that pools storage from multiple servers into a single large network file system.
enrichment.seo_title: GlusterFS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GlusterFS review 2026: how this open-source distributed file system works, its history with Red Hat, current project status, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GlusterFS is an open-source, scale-out distributed file system that aggregates storage capacity and disk resources from many commodity servers into one large parallel network file system, accessible over Ethernet or InfiniBand RDMA interconnects. It was created to let organizations build petabyte-scale storage pools without proprietary storage-area-network hardware, and it has historically been used in cloud computing infrastructure, streaming media services and content delivery networks that need large, horizontally scalable file storage. The project traces back to 2005, when Gluster Inc. was founded by Anand Babu Periasamy, who wrote the initial version of GlusterFS as the company's flagship product. Gluster operated as an independent storage startup for several years before Red Hat acquired it on 7 October 2011 for a reported 136 million US dollars, folding GlusterFS into Red Hat's broader storage strategy. Following the acquisition, Red Hat announced Red Hat Storage Server in June 2012 as a commercially supported distribution of GlusterFS bundled with Red Hat Enterprise Linux, and after Red Hat's 2014 acquisition of Inktank Storage, the company behind the competing Ceph file system, Red Hat rebranded its GlusterFS-based product to Red Hat Gluster Storage in April 2014 to distinguish it from its Ceph-based offering. For roughly a decade, GlusterFS benefited from Red Hat's engineering investment, corporate backing and enterprise support channel, which drove more than a thousand commits per year to the upstream project during its most active period. That changed at the end of 2024: Red Hat Gluster Storage's commercial support lifecycle officially ended on 31 December 2024, Red Hat disbanded its dedicated Gluster engineering team, and the company is no longer contributing development resources to the project. Commit activity dropped sharply as a result, falling to 78 commits in 2023 and just 31 commits in 2024, a fraction of the project's historical pace, and the broader ecosystem has responded by winding down GlusterFS integrations, with projects like TrueNAS SCALE removing GlusterFS backend support and other platforms such as Proxmox and QEMU debating whether to drop it as well. Despite this, GlusterFS has not been formally discontinued: it remains under community maintenance, with the 10.x and 11.x release lines still receiving occasional minor updates as of late 2025, and a segment of its user base continues to run it in production for cost, familiarity or architectural reasons even as newer options gain ground. Architecturally, GlusterFS has no centralized metadata server, which distinguishes it from many other distributed file systems; instead it uses an elastic hashing algorithm to locate data across storage bricks, avoiding a single point of failure or metadata bottleneck. Core capabilities include replication across nodes for redundancy, geo-replication for disaster recovery across sites, striping and distribution of files across bricks for scale, snapshotting, and support for both a native GlusterFS client protocol and standard NFS or SMB access for compatibility with existing infrastructure. GlusterFS is free software, licensed with core components under the GNU General Public License version 3, while some parts are dual-licensed under GPL version 2 or the GNU Lesser General Public License version 3, meaning any organization can download, deploy and modify it at no cost. There is no vendor-imposed pricing tier because there is no longer an official commercial product built on it from Red Hat; the practical cost to an adopter is entirely the commodity server hardware, storage media and systems-administration time needed to build and operate a cluster, with community forums, mailing lists and GitHub issues serving as the primary support channel in place of paid enterprise support. GlusterFS's target audience today is narrower than it was during its Red Hat-backed heyday: it appeals to organizations already running it in production that value stability and low migration risk, to hobbyists and homelab operators building distributed storage on commodity hardware, and to teams in cost-sensitive environments that need scale-out file storage without licensing fees. For new deployments, GlusterFS increasingly competes with and is being displaced by alternatives such as Ceph, which absorbed much of Red Hat's storage engineering attention after the Inktank acquisition and remains actively developed, as well as newer distributed and object storage systems like MinIO and cloud-native storage layers for Kubernetes such as Longhorn and Rook. GlusterFS's main differentiator historically was its comparatively simple, metadata-server-free architecture and ease of adding capacity by simply adding bricks, but its post-2024 reduced development velocity is now the central factor prospective adopters weigh against those architectural strengths.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GlusterFS is an open-source, scale-out distributed file system that pools storage from multiple commodity servers into a single large network file system, accessible over Ethernet or InfiniBand. It was created in 2005 by Gluster Inc., founded by Anand Babu Periasamy, and was acquired by Red Hat in October 2011 for a reported 136 million US dollars.

Red Hat sold it commercially as Red Hat Storage Server and later Red Hat Gluster Storage, but ended commercial support and disbanded its Gluster engineering team at the close of 2024. The project continues under reduced community maintenance rather than active corporate development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GlusterFS has no centralized metadata server; it uses an elastic hashing algorithm to locate files across storage bricks, avoiding a single point of failure or metadata bottleneck as the cluster scales. Capacity is added simply by attaching new bricks to a volume.

The system supports replication for redundancy, geo-replication for cross-site disaster recovery, striping and distribution of files across nodes, snapshotting, and access via its native client, NFS, or SMB for compatibility with existing tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GlusterFS is free, open-source software licensed primarily under GPLv3, with some components dual-licensed under GPLv2 or LGPLv3. There is no license fee to download, deploy or modify it.

Since Red Hat wound down its commercial Red Hat Gluster Storage product and support offering at the end of 2024, there is no official paid enterprise tier; adopters rely on community support channels and bear only their own hardware and administration costs.

faqs.question: Is GlusterFS still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only through community effort. Red Hat ended commercial support for Red Hat Gluster Storage on 31 December 2024 and disbanded its engineering team, and upstream commit activity has fallen sharply since, though minor releases on the 10.x and 11.x lines continue.

faqs.question: Is GlusterFS free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, GlusterFS is free, open-source software licensed under GPLv3 with some components dual-licensed under GPLv2 or LGPLv3. There is no cost to download, deploy or modify it.

faqs.question: Who created GlusterFS?
faqs.answer: GlusterFS was created in 2005 by Gluster Inc., founded by Anand Babu Periasamy, who wrote the initial version. Red Hat acquired Gluster in October 2011 for a reported 136 million US dollars.

faqs.question: What is the difference between GlusterFS and Red Hat Gluster Storage?
faqs.answer: GlusterFS is the open-source upstream project. Red Hat Gluster Storage was Red Hat's commercially supported distribution built on GlusterFS, but that commercial product's support lifecycle ended in December 2024.

faqs.question: Should I still deploy new GlusterFS clusters in 2026?
faqs.answer: Many teams now evaluate alternatives such as Ceph or Kubernetes-native storage systems for new deployments given GlusterFS's reduced development pace, though existing GlusterFS users continue running it in production.

faqs.question: Does GlusterFS require a centralized metadata server?
faqs.answer: No. GlusterFS uses an elastic hashing algorithm to locate data across storage bricks instead of a centralized metadata server, which avoids that architecture's single point of failure.

enrichment.seo_title: GNUnet Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GNUnet review 2026: how this GNU peer-to-peer networking framework works, its history, GNU Name System, file sharing, pricing (its free), pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GNUnet is a free software framework for secure, decentralized peer-to-peer networking that deliberately avoids any centralized or trusted intermediary. Rather than being a single application, it is a protocol stack and set of libraries on top of which developers build privacy-preserving applications, and the project ships several first-party applications of its own, including censorship-resistant file sharing, secure messaging, a virtual private network, and the GNU Name System, a decentralized replacement for DNS. GNUnet became an official GNU package on 17 February 2002, when Richard Stallman designated it as such, and it has been developed primarily under the leadership of Christian Grothoff ever since, rooted heavily in academic networking and cryptography research. The project began with a relatively narrow goal, anonymous and censorship-resistant file sharing, but over more than two decades it has grown into a much broader set of generic building blocks for secure networking applications, adding messaging, naming and other subsystems along the way. As a GNU project developed largely by volunteers, academics and grant-funded contributors rather than a venture-backed company, GNUnet has no traditional funding rounds or ownership history to speak of; its development has instead been supported by organizations such as NLnet, which funds free and open-source infrastructure projects, and by the academic institutions where core contributors like Grothoff have worked. Interest in GNUnet and similar decentralized, surveillance-resistant tools grew notably after the 2013 PRISM revelations about mass government surveillance programs, which brought new attention and contributors to peer-to-peer privacy projects across the free-software world. Technically, GNUnet's architecture centers on a modular set of subsystems that communicate over an encrypted, authenticated peer-to-peer overlay network, with peer discovery and routing handled through mechanisms including its R5N distributed hash table and overlay routing designed to resist traffic analysis. The CADET subsystem sits at the heart of the stack, providing secure end-to-end encrypted channels between peers on the overlay network; a command-line tool lets a user open a port on a local machine that another peer can connect to for chat or file transfer, similar in spirit to a privacy-preserving alternative to raw TCP sockets. On top of this transport layer, GNUnet's file-sharing application was originally built purely for anonymous publishing and retrieval, letting a publisher trade off performance against anonymity, though over time the project has also added support for non-anonymous file sharing when better performance is the priority. The GNU Name System, one of GNUnet's most distinctive components, replaces the hierarchical, centrally-rooted structure of traditional DNS with a directed graph of delegations between users, so that name resolution does not depend on any central registrar or root server; queries and responses are cryptographically secured so that even peers relaying a lookup cannot easily see what is being resolved, giving GNS both integrity and query privacy that conventional DNS and even DNS-over-HTTPS cannot match. Because GNUnet is entirely free and open-source software released without any commercial licensing tier, there is no pricing structure in the conventional sense: anyone can download the source, compile or install pre-built packages, and run a full GNUnet peer at no cost, with the project's own documentation and mailing lists serving as the primary support channel rather than a paid enterprise tier. The project's own current status notes acknowledge that some subsystems, including CADET and the file-sharing application, have known moderate performance and usability limitations as of recent releases, reflecting its nature as an evolving research-driven project rather than a hardened, commercially polished consumer product. GNUnet's target audience is accordingly narrower and more technical than mainstream privacy tools: it appeals most to systems researchers, cryptography and networking academics, free-software and digital-rights activists building censorship-resistant infrastructure, and developers who want to build custom peer-to-peer applications on top of a security-audited, GNU-endorsed protocol stack rather than rolling their own networking layer. Competitively, GNUnet is often mentioned alongside other decentralized and anonymity-focused networking projects such as Tor, I2P, and IPFS, but it differentiates itself by aiming to be a general-purpose application framework rather than a single-purpose anonymity network or content-addressed storage system; where Tor focuses on anonymous routing for existing internet traffic and IPFS focuses on content addressing and distribution, GNUnet's ambition is to provide the full stack, from peer discovery and encrypted transport up through naming and application-layer services, needed to build an alternative, decentralized internet resistant to centralized control or mass surveillance.

faqs.answer: Tor focuses on anonymous routing for existing internet traffic through a network of relays, while GNUnet aims to be a broader peer-to-peer application framework covering encrypted transport, file sharing, messaging and decentralized naming from the ground up.

faqs.question: What is the GNU Name System?
faqs.answer: The GNU Name System (GNS) is GNUnet's decentralized alternative to DNS, replacing the centralized DNS hierarchy with a directed graph of user delegations and cryptographically secured, private queries.

enrichment.short_description: GoAccess is a free, open-source, real-time web log analyzer that runs in a terminal or browser to turn Apache, Nginx and cloud server logs into interactive statistics.
enrichment.seo_title: GoAccess Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoAccess review 2026: how this open-source real-time web log analyzer works, its terminal and browser dashboards, pricing (its free), features, pros, cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GoAccess is a free, open-source real-time web log analyzer designed to run either as an interactive dashboard inside a terminal on Unix-like systems or as a self-contained HTML report viewable in a web browser. Its core purpose is to take raw access logs from web servers and cloud services and turn them into readable, navigable statistics without requiring a database, a background server process, or an external analytics service, making it a favorite among system administrators and DevOps engineers who want fast, actionable HTTP insight directly from the command line. The tool was created by Gerardo Orellana, who published the first version of GoAccess in July 2010, and the project's copyright notices, which span from 2009 through to recent releases, reflect over a decade and a half of continuous, largely single-maintainer-driven development. Unlike many other tools in the log-analytics and web-analytics space, GoAccess has never been a venture-backed company, has no funding rounds, acquisitions or corporate ownership history, and remains a community open-source project maintained primarily by its original author with contributions from the broader developer community on GitHub. Architecturally, GoAccess is written in the C programming language and depends on very few external libraries, most notably ncurses for its terminal-based interface, which keeps it lightweight and fast even when processing very large log files. It can parse the log file formats produced by a wide range of web servers and services, including Apache, Nginx, Amazon S3 access logs, and Amazon CloudFront logs, and it supports both static, one-time analysis of an existing log file and real-time, continuously updating analysis of a log as new requests are appended to it. When run in terminal mode, GoAccess presents a curses-based dashboard broken into panels covering metrics like unique visitors, requested files, static requests, referrers, 404 errors, operating systems, browsers, and geographic location of visitors when combined with an IP geolocation database, all navigable with keyboard shortcuts directly over an SSH session. When run with its HTML output option, GoAccess instead generates a single, self-contained HTML report using embedded JavaScript and CSS that can optionally connect back to a running GoAccess process over WebSocket for live-updating charts and tables in a browser, which is particularly useful for teams that want a shareable visual report without granting shell access to a server. Core features include support for custom log formats via configurable format strings, the ability to combine multiple log files or read compressed logs directly, IP-to-country and IP-to-city geolocation when paired with a MaxMind GeoIP database, visitor and bot filtering, and exportable JSON output for feeding GoAccess's parsed data into other tooling or pipelines. Because GoAccess is free, open-source software with no commercial edition, subscription tier, or hosted SaaS product, there is no pricing structure to speak of in the traditional sense: any organization or individual can install it via most Linux package managers, Homebrew on macOS, or by compiling from source, and run it at no cost indefinitely, with community support available through its GitHub repository, mailing list, and official documentation rather than a paid support contract. This zero-cost, self-hosted, privacy-respecting model is central to GoAccess's appeal, since it processes logs entirely on infrastructure the operator already controls, sending no data to any third party, which distinguishes it sharply from cloud-based web analytics products that require embedding a JavaScript tracking snippet and routing visitor data through a third-party service. GoAccess's target audience is primarily technical: system administrators managing one or a handful of web servers who want a quick, no-fuss way to see traffic patterns and error rates without standing up a full analytics stack, DevOps and SRE teams who want lightweight, real-time visibility into request volume and latency-adjacent metrics during incidents, and website operators who prefer log-based analytics that avoid client-side JavaScript trackers and the accuracy loss caused by ad blockers. Competitively, GoAccess sits in a niche adjacent to both server log analyzers like AWStats and Webalizer, which it has largely displaced due to its real-time capability and modern interface, and privacy-focused web analytics products like Plausible, Fathom, or GoatCounter, which instead rely on lightweight JavaScript tracking rather than server log parsing. GoAccess's key differentiator is that it needs no tracking script, no database, and almost no setup beyond pointing it at an existing log file, which makes it especially attractive to privacy-conscious operators and anyone who wants traffic insight from infrastructure they already run rather than adding a new third-party dependency to their site.

enrichment.short_description: GoatCounter is an open-source, privacy-friendly web analytics tool that tracks page views without cookies or personal data, available hosted or self-hosted.
enrichment.seo_title: GoatCounter Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoatCounter review 2026: how this open-source, cookieless web analytics tool works, its free and paid hosted plans, self-hosting option, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GoatCounter is an open-source, privacy-focused web analytics platform built as a lightweight alternative to Google Analytics and Matomo, designed to give website owners meaningful visitor statistics without tracking individuals or collecting personal data. It was created by Martin Tournoij, a developer who publishes under the handle arp242 and has a background in accessibility-focused open-source work, and the hosted goatcounter.com service launched in August 2019 as a single-developer project rather than a venture-funded startup. Because GoatCounter has no outside investors, its history is one of organic, developer-led growth rather than funding rounds or acquisitions, and it remains funded primarily through hosted-service revenue and GitHub Sponsors contributions from users who want to support server costs beyond what free usage covers. Technically, GoatCounter is a single, self-contained binary written in the Go programming language, which makes self-hosting unusually simple compared to analytics platforms that require a separate database server, message queue, or multi-service container stack; an operator can download one binary, run it, and have a working analytics backend. The hosted version at goatcounter.com offers the same software as a managed service so that non-technical users do not need to run their own server. On the client side, GoatCounter can be integrated in several ways: a small JavaScript snippet (roughly 3.5 kilobytes) embedded on a page, a JavaScript-free tracking pixel for sites that cannot or do not want to run scripts, server-side or backend middleware integrations, or bulk import from existing log files, giving site owners flexibility depending on their technical constraints and privacy posture. Its privacy model is a core differentiator: GoatCounter does not use cookies, does not assign persistent unique identifiers to visitors, and is designed to be usable under GDPR without requiring a cookie-consent banner in most jurisdictions, since it avoids the kind of persistent personal-data tracking that triggers consent requirements. Despite this privacy-by-design approach, it still surfaces the metrics most site owners actually want: page views, unique visits (computed without cookies via short-lived, privacy-respecting session heuristics), referrers, campaign and UTM tracking, browser and screen-size breakdowns, and visitor country and language, all presented through a dashboard that the project has deliberately built to be usable with screen readers and keyboard navigation, reflecting the accessibility focus of its creator. Data ownership is another emphasized principle: users can export all of their collected data at any time and cancel their account whenever they choose, with no lock-in to the hosted platform since the same open-source code can be self-hosted instead. GoatCounter's pricing follows a freemium model on the hosted service: goatcounter.com is offered free of charge for reasonable public and non-commercial usage, explicitly covering personal websites and small-to-medium businesses, while sites sending very high volumes of traffic, such as millions of page views per day, or using the service for larger commercial purposes are asked to either contribute financially through GitHub Sponsors or move to a paid arrangement to help cover the hosting costs of running the shared service; for organizations that want full control and no usage ceiling at all, the self-hosted option remains completely free since it is the same open-source codebase, distributed under the European Union Public License version 1.2, a license permissive enough that a self-hosted deployment can even be modified and shipped commercially as a fork provided the same license terms are preserved. GoatCounter's target audience is squarely privacy-conscious individuals and small teams: independent bloggers and personal website owners who want basic traffic insight without adopting Google Analytics, small businesses that need GDPR-friendly analytics without legal overhead, open-source and privacy-minded developers who want to self-host their own analytics stack on a single Go binary, and accessibility-focused organizations that specifically want an analytics dashboard usable by screen-reader users. Competitively, GoatCounter sits in the same privacy-analytics category as Plausible, Fathom Analytics and Simple Analytics, but differentiates itself primarily through its self-hosting simplicity as a single Go binary, its unusually strong accessibility focus, its permissive EUPL open-source license that allows commercial forking, and its founder-led, donation-supported model rather than the venture-backed SaaS structure of some competitors, making it a natural choice for developers who value transparency and control over polish and enterprise sales support.

enrichment.short_description: GoCardless is a London-founded fintech platform that lets businesses collect one-off and recurring payments via direct debit and bank-to-bank transfer across 40-plus countries.
enrichment.seo_title: GoCardless Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoCardless review 2026: how this direct debit and bank payment platform works, transaction fee pricing tiers, funding history, features, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GoCardless is a fintech payments platform built specifically around recurring and one-off bank-to-bank payments, most notably direct debit, letting subscription businesses, membership organizations, billing platforms and other companies collect payments directly from customer bank accounts rather than relying solely on card networks. The company was founded in 2011 by Hiroki Takeuchi, Matt Robinson and Tom Blomfield, who were students at Oxford University at the time; the founders pitched the idea to Y Combinator in early 2011 and secured 150,000 US dollars in funding within hours of their pitch, giving GoCardless one of the more well-known Y Combinator origin stories in UK fintech. The company is headquartered in London and has grown from a UK-focused direct debit tool into an international payments platform. GoCardless's funding history reflects a steady, well-capitalized growth trajectory rather than a single blockbuster raise. In 2012, the company closed 1.5 million pounds from investors including Y Combinator, Google Ventures, Salesforce and Accel Partners. In 2014, a 7 million US dollar Series B led by Balderton Capital funded expansion into France, Germany and Spain. A 13 million US dollar round followed in March 2016, and a 22.5 million US dollar Series D in September 2017 brought total funding to 47.2 million US dollars at that point. The company continued scaling internationally, expanding into Australia and New Zealand in 2020 alongside a 95 million US dollar raise that pushed its valuation past 1 billion US dollars, making it a fintech unicorn with Alphabet's growth-investing arm among its backers. Its largest disclosed round came on 8 February 2022, a 312 million US dollar Series G led by Permira. Most recently, in December 2025, Dutch payments company Mollie announced an agreement to acquire GoCardless for approximately 1.05 billion euro in a mostly stock-based deal, combining the two companies into a payments group valued at roughly 3 billion euro, marking a consolidation moment for the European payments infrastructure sector. Technically, GoCardless operates as a payments infrastructure layer that businesses integrate via API or pre-built plugins and connectors, covering direct debit schemes across the UK (Bacs), the eurozone (SEPA), the United States (ACH), Australia (BECS) and other markets, along with card payments and open-banking-based instant bank payments in some regions, giving merchants a single integration point for multiple local bank-payment rails rather than needing separate integrations per country. Its platform includes features such as Success+, a machine-learning-driven payment retry and recovery system designed to automatically recapture failed recurring payments, and Protect+, a fraud detection and chargeback-management layer available on higher plans. Core capabilities include support for one-off, subscription, membership and instalment payment collection, a customizable hosted checkout, webhooks and an extensive library of over 350 pre-built integrations with accounting, CRM and billing platforms such as Xero, QuickBooks, Salesforce and Zoho, and coverage spanning more than 40 countries for supported payment methods. GoCardless prices itself on a transaction-fee basis rather than a flat subscription, structured across four tiers. The Standard plan charges 1 percent plus 20 pence per domestic UK transaction, capped at 4 pounds, with no monthly or setup fees. The Advanced plan raises the fee to 1.25 percent plus 20 pence, capped at 5 pounds, and adds the Success+ automatic payment recovery feature, which the company states can recover up to 70 percent of failed payments, along with enhanced identity verification. The Pro plan charges 1.4 percent plus 20 pence, capped at 5.60 pounds, and layers in the Protect+ fraud detection and chargeback-challenge support on top of everything in Advanced. International transactions carry higher fees across all tiers, roughly double the domestic percentage plus the same 20 pence flat fee, and transactions over 2,000 pounds incur an additional 0.3 percent surcharge regardless of tier. For businesses processing more than 1 million pounds in annual revenue, GoCardless offers a Custom plan with negotiated volume discounts and dedicated account support. Optional add-ons include custom branding on bank statements for 50 pounds per month and a fully customized checkout experience for 150 pounds per month. GoCardless's target audience is primarily subscription-based and recurring-revenue businesses: SaaS companies, membership and subscription-box services, utilities, education providers, and B2B billing platforms that need to reduce payment failure rates and processing costs compared to card-only checkout flows, since bank-to-bank debit typically carries lower failure and chargeback rates than cards for recurring billing. Competitively, GoCardless is most often compared with Stripe, which offers direct debit support as one payment method among many within a much broader payments and billing platform, as well as more narrowly focused direct debit specialists. GoCardless differentiates itself by focusing specifically on bank-payment rails and recurring billing recovery rather than being a general-purpose payments processor, and its pending acquisition by Mollie signals a broader industry move toward consolidated, pan-European payments platforms that combine card acquiring, bank payments and recurring billing under one roof.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GoCardless is a London-headquartered fintech platform built around collecting recurring and one-off payments directly from customer bank accounts, most notably via direct debit, rather than relying solely on card networks. It was founded in 2011 by Hiroki Takeuchi, Matt Robinson and Tom Blomfield, Oxford University students who secured 150,000 US dollars from Y Combinator within hours of their initial pitch.

The company raised well-known rounds over the following decade, including a 2022 Series G of 312 million US dollars led by Permira, and in December 2025 Dutch payments company Mollie announced an agreement to acquire GoCardless for approximately 1.05 billion euro, forming a combined payments group valued at roughly 3 billion euro.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GoCardless connects businesses to direct debit and bank-payment schemes across the UK, eurozone, United States, Australia and other markets through a single API integration, alongside card payments and open-banking instant bank payments in some regions. Its Success+ feature uses machine learning to automatically retry and recover failed recurring payments, while Protect+ adds fraud detection and chargeback-challenge support on higher tiers.

The platform supports one-off, subscription, membership and instalment payment collection, offers a customizable hosted checkout, and connects to more than 350 pre-built integrations with accounting, CRM and billing tools such as Xero, QuickBooks and Salesforce.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GoCardless charges per-transaction fees rather than a flat subscription, across four tiers. Standard costs 1 percent plus 20 pence per domestic transaction (capped at 4 pounds); Advanced costs 1.25 percent plus 20 pence (capped at 5 pounds) and adds Success+ payment recovery; Pro costs 1.4 percent plus 20 pence (capped at 5.60 pounds) and adds Protect+ fraud protection.

International transactions cost roughly double the domestic percentage across all tiers, and payments over 2,000 pounds add a 0.3 percent surcharge. Businesses processing over 1 million pounds annually can access a negotiated Custom plan, and optional add-ons include custom bank-statement branding and a fully customized checkout.

enrichment.short_description: GoCD is a free, open-source continuous delivery server from Thoughtworks that models complex build, test and deployment pipelines with a value-stream map view.
enrichment.seo_title: GoCD Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GoCD review 2026: how this open-source continuous delivery server from Thoughtworks works, its history from Cruise, pricing (its free), features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GoCD is a free, open-source continuous delivery and release-automation server used by software teams to model and run complex build, test and deployment pipelines, with a signature value-stream map view that shows an entire path to production in a single screen. Its origins trace back to 2007, when Thoughtworks Studios began developing a commercial continuous integration and release product internally named Cruise, conceived as an evolution of CruiseControl, one of the earliest continuous integration servers in the industry. Cruise was renamed GoCD (often referred to simply as Go) around 2010 as Thoughtworks repositioned the product, and it remained a closed-source, commercially licensed product for several more years before the company made a strategic decision to open it up. On 25 February 2014, Thoughtworks announced that GoCD would be released as open-source software under the Apache License, Version 2.0, converting what had been a paid enterprise CD tool into freely available software that any team could download, self-host and modify at no cost. For roughly the following six to seven years, Thoughtworks continued sponsoring GoCD's development as an open-source project associated with its broader Thoughtworks Studios product line and its well-known Technology Radar publication, which has repeatedly assessed CD tooling trends. However, since January 2021, GoCD has had no dedicated organizational backing behind its development and no commercial or enterprise support offering; it continues today purely as a community-maintained, Thoughtworks-sponsored open-source project, with Thoughtworks providing infrastructure and light sponsorship rather than active, funded engineering investment. Because GoCD has always been either an internal corporate product or an open-source project rather than an independent company, it has no funding-round or acquisition history of its own in the way a startup would; its financial story is entirely tied to Thoughtworks's decision to first commercialize and then open-source the tool. Architecturally, GoCD operates as a central CD server, typically deployed with a primary server component and one or more agent processes that execute pipeline stages, and its core functionality, including value-stream visualization, workflow modeling and traceability, is built in rather than dependent on plugins, distinguishing it from lighter CI tools that require heavy plugin stacking to achieve similar pipeline modeling. GoCD's most distinctive feature is its value-stream map, a single visual representation of the entire path from a code commit through every build, test and deployment stage to production, letting teams spot bottlenecks or failures at a glance rather than piecing together status across disconnected job views. It supports sophisticated pipeline modeling with parallel execution, fan-in and fan-out dependency management, and the ability to compare artifacts, files and commit messages across any two arbitrary builds for deep traceability during debugging or audits. GoCD integrates with cloud-native and containerized environments including Kubernetes, Docker and AWS, and its extensible plugin architecture supports a curated set of official and community plugins alongside custom plugin development for source control systems, artifact stores, authentication providers, and notification channels. Because GoCD is entirely open-source software with no commercial tier, there is no pricing structure at all in the conventional SaaS sense: teams download the server and agent binaries or container images at no cost, run them on their own infrastructure, and rely on community documentation, GitHub issues and forums for support rather than a paid support contract, since Thoughtworks no longer offers commercial support for the product. GoCD's target audience today is technical teams that specifically need sophisticated pipeline modeling and full-path traceability for complex, multi-stage release processes, particularly organizations with intricate dependency chains between services that a simpler linear CI pipeline tool struggles to represent clearly, as well as teams already invested in GoCD from its Thoughtworks-backed years who value stability and want to avoid a migration. Competitively, GoCD sits alongside other open-source and self-hosted CI/CD servers such as Jenkins, and more recently against cloud-native, config-as-code pipeline tools such as GitLab CI/CD, GitHub Actions, CircleCI and Argo CD for Kubernetes-native deployments. Its main differentiator has historically been the value-stream map and native, plugin-free pipeline modeling and traceability, but its lack of active organizational backing since 2021 is now the central trade-off prospective adopters weigh against tools like Jenkins, GitLab or GitHub Actions that continue to receive substantial, well-funded ongoing development and a much larger plugin and integration ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GoCD is a free, open-source continuous delivery server used to model and run complex build, test and deployment pipelines. It originated at Thoughtworks Studios in 2007 as a commercial product named Cruise, an evolution of the earlier CruiseControl continuous integration server, and was renamed GoCD around 2010.

Thoughtworks open-sourced GoCD under the Apache License, Version 2.0 on 25 February 2014. Since January 2021, the project has had no dedicated organizational backing or commercial support offering, continuing purely as a community-maintained project sponsored by Thoughtworks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GoCD's signature feature is its value-stream map, a single visual representation of the entire path from a code commit through every build, test and deployment stage to production. Pipeline modeling supports parallel execution and complex fan-in and fan-out dependency management, all built in rather than requiring heavy plugin stacking.

The platform provides traceability that lets teams compare files, artifacts and commit messages across any two arbitrary builds, integrates with Kubernetes, Docker and AWS for cloud-native deployment, and supports an extensible plugin architecture for source control, artifact storage, authentication and notifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: GoCD is entirely free and open-source, licensed under Apache 2.0, with no commercial tier or paid subscription. Teams download the server and agent binaries or container images and run them on their own infrastructure at no cost.
There is no paid support contract available, since Thoughtworks no longer offers commercial support for GoCD; users rely on community documentation, GitHub issues and forums instead.

faqs.question: Is GoCD free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, GoCD is completely free and open-source software licensed under Apache 2.0, with no commercial tier or subscription fee.

faqs.question: Who created GoCD?
faqs.answer: GoCD originated at Thoughtworks Studios in 2007 as a commercial product named Cruise, an evolution of the CruiseControl continuous integration server, and was renamed GoCD around 2010 before being open-sourced in February 2014.

faqs.question: Is GoCD still actively developed?
faqs.answer: GoCD has had no dedicated organizational backing or commercial support since January 2021, and it continues today as a community-maintained project sponsored by Thoughtworks rather than an actively funded product.

faqs.question: What makes GoCD different from Jenkins?
faqs.answer: GoCD's built-in value-stream map and native complex pipeline modeling and cross-build traceability come out of the box, whereas Jenkins typically relies on a much larger plugin ecosystem to achieve comparable pipeline visualization.

faqs.question: Does GoCD support Kubernetes and Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, GoCD integrates with Kubernetes, Docker and AWS, letting teams run pipeline agents and deployments in cloud-native and containerized environments.

faqs.question: Does GoCD offer a hosted or managed version?
faqs.answer: No, GoCD is only available as self-hosted, open-source software; there is no official managed cloud offering from Thoughtworks.
